title: Matthew Halton Remembered By Colleagues
rights: public domain (presumed; pre-1963 broadcast, no rights statement on archive.org item)
transcript: He argued with Gering, he interviewed Churchill, Neville Chamberlain, half a score of French primers. He talked to Haile Selassie about the tragedy of Ethiopia. He questioned Maxime Lietvenoff about the Soviet intentions and he wrote and wrote. One of the editors who looked forward to his dispatches was George Rogers. Matthew Houghton was probably the most brilliant foreign correspondent Canada ever produced. To a whole generation of Canadians he was the most eloquent chronicleer of our time, a man who could capture the sweep in the drama of history, and television simple, majestic language, any Canadian could understand. He had come from the small foothills hamlet of Pinscher Creek and Alberta, a solemn, earnest young man of the burning mission to be a great reporter. When he died last week, he could be secure in the knowledge that his country had produced none greater. For 23 years, Halton roamed Europe and Africa and Asia, interviewing potentates and peasants, the great and the near-great, the men of peace and the men of war, and from his typewriter float an enormous stream of words, some four million he estimated recently. He illuminated the great events of an era in a manner so vivid and sharp that all who read him could feel themselves to be eyewitnesses of history. We are proud to have presented in the pages of the Star Weekly much of Matthew Holtman's phenomenal output. In one of his articles two years ago, he wrote, I have not been everywhere and seen everything, but I have been to most countries and seen much. I have been to Carcasson and Karnak and Persepolis and Ceylon and Darjeeling. And I have seen so many of the world's famous places in astonishing and dramatic circumstances. Canadian journalism may never see his lake again. In 1934, Matthew Halton opened a report with a single short sentence. Germany means war, he said. He was a man of peace. And when the war he had foreseen did come, he had to think and write about something that outraged every instinct in him. But it was his job, and he did it magnificently. He saw Finland crushed, and he covered the desert war. And then in 1943, he became Matthew Halton of the CBC, and his voice became a vital link between Canadians at home and their men in Europe. He was with them as they stormed the rugged hills of Italy and crossed the countless rivers. He was with the men who landed in Normandy in 1944. Charles Lynch, now a CBC correspondent himself, was in Normandy too. Matt Houghton was a veteran correspondent by the time D-Day came around in 1944, and I was the greenest man on the beach. He took the trouble to befriend the green horn and put things into perspective, and he did it with high humor and good fellowship. During the campaign, after breakfast, he'd say, well, let's go face death. And he faced it and reported it, and after the fighting was over, he worked as intensely as ever covering the peace. His curiosity was boundless, and his enthusiasm was as lively as his dislikes. I doubt very much if Matt Holton ever turned out a dull piece in his life. In latter years I knew him chiefly through his letters, which were always an event when they arrived. Sometimes he was on a high flying cloud, sometimes his ballad was of the bar room kind. Now he's taken from us before his time, and will miss his letters, his broadcasts, and his visits. This phased death, he said, and in some of his reports you could hear death all around. It was in Normandy that he drove up to a forward observation post during the attack on Carpeque Airfield. Let's listen again to Matthew Horton as he spoke then. We were mortgared getting in there. Sometimes the shells fell far away, and they're coming sounded like the London Air Raids, Sometimes they fell a few yards away and came with a sobbing scream. Before five o'clock, at first light, we could see the hangers on Carpike Iairdrow and the village. For the next hour we saw hardly anything. Within a few minutes after the barrage started, the objective below us and the fields were a dense cloud of smoke and cordite fumes. In the following recording, you will hear German and Canadian machine guns and German shells at Pell honors, and my attempt to describe something of what I saw, and above all the incessant earth-shaking and atrocious pounding of the hundreds of guns. Fortunately for you, the sounds as recorded on a disc are quiet and tame compared to what they are when we hear them. Even bursting shells that shook us and blasted us sound pretty tame on a disc. Listen to this, and then imagine it at least ten times as loud. And now twenty-five minutes have passed. Twenty-five minutes of the most... I think the most ferocious barrage I've ever seen. When I first spoke, we could see the hangars, and we could see the air drum in the first light of dawn. We could see the Germans' wrong point. Now, there's nothing but the fog of war, and to be on this battlefield, one understands the meaning of the phrase, the fog of war. I sit here, or I stand here in this observation post and all time with two or three Canadian officers, two or three Canadian privates, and watch and wonder. All we can see is smoke and the terrible burst of flame as one of the huge naval shells on the enemy position. Occasionally an enemy shell falls near us. And you can hear, as I speak, you can hear the chattering, the tired stuffing of the machine gun. And now I can see German flares going up. But the nervy German, he must be nervy now under barrage of scores, no less than scores of thousands of shells, sending up his flares. So apparently he's still thinking. As I said that I could see tracer bullets from our machine guns going across our front. Now I can hear other machine guns farther away as if from our troops who are advancing. Some of our tanks are going across the front too. So this is the morning. This is the afternoon that was bad enough. This is the morning we waited for. A morning in plan. A morning in in which the fairfields of Normandy are torn and rent and split apart. It was grim, yes, but not all of it, because that was impossible, both for the men who fought and the men who wrote. Don Fairburn is another war correspondent who remembers. It was a quiet moonlight night around the Canadian press camp, which consisted of a few tents in an apple orchard. Action at the front had been limited that day, and a few of us were relaxing over a card game. A game of chance in which the stakes were not high, but confusing because of the variety of money in use. A few intrepid souls had managed to smuggle in some English currency. There were no Canadian dollars, but there were French francs on which nobody could place a positive value in those days and occupation francs, which did have a definite value in our paybooks at least. For light, there was one small electric bulb run from a gas-powered generator. There had been sporadic artillery and anti-aircraft fire, but it didn't disturb the play, until suddenly a German aircraft dropped something on an ammunition dump close by. The light went out, and players, cards, and stakes scattered toward what little shelter there was in the tent. After a time as the night grew silent again, There came a voice from under a table. It was Matt Haltman saying, I have three sevens and the butt of a candle. He went through it all, the hard parts, and the parts you could recall with a laugh afterwards. But of all his reports, the one he liked best himself was the day he could tell us that the nightmare was ended. The German war is over. Five little words that one hardly dares to speak. During long weary years and during hours that seem like years, one sometimes wondered if the carnival of death wasn't a nightmare. And now that the nightmare's over, one has to what it isn't a pleasant dream from which we'll wake, to find the usual mad mornings of war and blood. men have grown old in a morning, gentlemen have grown calloused on slaughter. The best blood of a civilization has been poured away like red wine in an orgy, and then someone tells you it's over, and your first thought is, no more Canadians will die. Not again in this war, will Canadians huddle against the wet earth of Europe in shuddering dons and And then rise to their feet and move out into the storm of steel. Not again shall we see brave men who can stand no more. No more mounds of earth beside the road. The war isn't officially over, but the time has come to be glad. Life can begin again. The anger of the guns has died away. Songs can be heard again. Spring can be spring. day the sun rises as it hasn't risen for nearly six years. And soldiers I've talked to don't quite know what to do about it. They shave and have breakfast. They clean their guns. They try to brush the mud off their clothes. They ask if there's any male. After all, they've lived strange, dangerous lives. It's hard to believe that no shells will come screaming over. It's hard to believe that if they stand up in the open, nobody will shoot at them. Death has walked to their side. It's hard to believe for a day or two that the nightmare is over and that they can drink the wine of life. Yes, it was over, but the piece that Matt Horton like all of us had longed for wasn't an easy one. There was still work for Matt Horton to do. Andrew Cowan recalls the post-war years. I worked closely with Matthew Halton for many years in the CBC's London offices. Closely, in as much as our offices, were within a few feet of each other. But like a good artist or craftsman, he was a solitary worker. When he wished to prepare a broadcast, he went into his office and shut the door. Alone with himself, he thought and wrote. He was an efficient worker. He assembled his clippings, his notes, his statistics before him, checked last minute data, and then began to type. Like most efficient workman, he went quietly about his job. He was so quiet about the office that most of the time we didn't know whether he was in or out. He never stormed about to relieve his feelings, or raised his voice in anger, or was rude with members of the office staff. Except on rare occasions in fair verbal fight, I never saw him rude with anyone. Boars and Yahoo's he ignored. He cultivated his own sources of information quietly and in his own way. He didn't care much for press conferences. But Matthew Holton was the very opposite to a lonely or aloof man. His friends were everywhere. Among his colleagues in the newspaper and broadcasting world, among writers, actors, artists, politicians, scientists, teachers, trade unionists, and people doing ordinary jobs. Matthew Hall never assumed that people doing ordinary jobs were just ordinary people. Quite literally, he walked with princes nor lost the common touch. He was a personal friend of the Duke of Edinburgh. He took pride in his friendship, so he never boasted of them. His contact with the great didn't make him cynical, and he never betrayed their trust. Yes, his friends were everywhere. Many of them in the British Broadcasting Corporation, because Matthew Holton's voice was almost as familiar to British listeners as it was to us. Winford von Thomas knew him well. I shall always remember Matt as the Canadian who of all the many gifted Canadians who've come to report the British scene, knew and understood us best. You never forgot his pride and love of Canada. That cropped up in his conversation on all possible occasions. And I still remember the sparkling his eye when he used to talk about the Canadian West and its possibilities. He teased me by the arm and say, look, you ought to get out there and see this thing for yourself. It's the biggest thing happening in the Commonwealth today. But he'd also come to feel himself completely a part of Britain and above all of London. He knew us in all our strengths and weaknesses. And in a wholehearted way, I think he'd almost come love are very false. I used to drive with him on many a third day down to a little group of journalists, editors, actors and people in the know about current affairs, who meet informally in the third day club in one of London's best known seafood restaurants. And Matt, by the way, was a connoisseur of oysters. When he was in his element care, he knew everybody worth knowing in London could always bring us the latest inside story. He had that superb journalists instinct for getting all the information he wanted from a man in the quickest and pleasant as possible way. And so at the Thursday Club, we all of us revel in sharing in the wealth of his knowledge about the strange passing show of our public life. And I remember the last time we drove down there together, I was looking out for the taxi window on the crowded narrow streets to which our car was struggling and I said to Matt, But doesn't this get you down occasionally? All this sprawl, this unplanned mess? And Matt gave me one of his irresistible, critical grins. Do you know he said, I wouldn't change it. I love it just as it is. It's got rare character. That indeed was what Matt had himself, rare character. Another of his friends was a man whose reputation for a stringent comment has made him National Institution in Britain, Gilbert Harding. The first time I met Matthew Holton in Canada, where I was rushing for the DBC during the war, I tried to quarrel with him. I failed. The reason was that he was a Canadian who understood the English. And what's more, he understood English when like me, the quarrel some kind. I think that from my point of view, that was his great secret. We exchanged quips, pincher, creek, albetter, I said to him, what an extraordinary place to come from. At least you remember what it was called, he said, where you, you come from Hereford, and we call your right face cattle perfect. I had a great respect and a liking for Matthew Holton. He was a reporter and a journalist of a competent kind and I am a man of no particular achievement. He was, I think, on the whole more at home in London than even I am. He made much of us, and much of the opportunities that London has to offer. We belonged even to the same club. Only one at a time because I had to leave one, I couldn't bear the back-slaping that met it in mind. If anybody slapped his back, he slapped back, which was more than I could. And now that he is dead, I remember with infinite regret that I was always his guest and never his host. And now it's too late. Another familiar British voice, Richard Dimpleby. It was after the war that I knew met simply as a family man, living happily with his wife and his two children, an ideal wife, and a boy and a girl of unusual charm composure. I have a photograph at home that taken that my daughter's christening and Matt is there, glass in hand as we drank a toast, beaming down at her. I think he must have lived a very full and contented life with his family in their home just below Hampstead Heath here in London. What wonderful summer evening parties they used to give on the terrace outside the drawing-room windows and on the lawn, when the heat and the noise of the day had gone and the trees were thick and dark around the garden and a gentle bubble of conversation rose into the dusk. It was there that you saw the breadth of Matt's friendships, writers and politicians, poets, newspaper men and broadcasters, and somehow I would find myself at the keyboard of the piano, and across section of the guests would gather round to shatter the evening piece with boisterous songs. And that was there everywhere. The perfect host, Witte, informed, interested in others of deep sympathy but of determined views. He had a great deal to do in life. He did part of it. The rest will never be achieved. And many people will will be the poorer. Matthew Holton, friend, host, husband, father. But always there was his work in many ways inseparable from the man himself. Another memory here from Norman de Poe. First time I met Matt Holton was in London well after the war, and when I was still relatively inexperienced as the editor who had to deal with his reports, My approach to him was some trepidation, a famous and polished, foreign correspondent and a very green editor, but really before anything could happen or we could talk to each other very much at all, someone else came into the office, and Matt Houghton turned and said, I'd like you to meet Norm DePole, he's my boss. Matt Houghton was like that. Looking at his reports as an editor, the thing that struck you was his ability to construct a colorful phrase that meant something. There's one that sticks in my memory especially. I don't recall exactly when he used it, but I know it was about one of those big post-war events that shaped history. The gale of the world, just a phrase, perhaps, but one of those phrases that Matt Halton could use to make you know that this was one of the hinges of history. There was always something there in every one of his reports, a paragraph, perhaps a sentence, perhaps only a phrase, but always something that cut down through the rush of events that were happening today, right down deep to those dark obscure forces that have troubled our time. The other thing about Matt was that he cared what was happening to humankind. I remember sitting with him in London one night, and we were talking about the Spanish Civil War, something that was already covered with the fine dust that history uses to dim old passions, and something that a whole generation of young Canadians knows only as a paragraph in a textbook. And yet, more than 15 years afterwards, you could hear the note of anguish in Matt Halten's voice as he recalled those days. He cared about human freedom. He cared about human dignity. And he cared about the simple human right to live a life at peace. And because he cared, he made us care, too. He was a many-sided man, impossible to sum up in a few words. One of his Canadian colleagues in London, though, thought that there was one part of Matt Holden that gave a basic clue to his character. Rudy Pelleche. The artist is perhaps the clue to this man's character and to the nature of his achievement. He was ever at a reflective kindly, but incisive and quiet. And his voice was doubtless the second clue, unmistakably Canadian, but somehow overlaid with the patterner of something European. And both these clues perhaps explain why he could, with grace, ease, and real achievement, the man born in Canada, who made London the home of his choice, and quite calmly took the world for his problems. Yes, the world was his province. King George VI awarded Matthew Holtan the Order of the British Empire. That and his doctorate from his old university were only two formal tributes to his life and work. And now he's gone. And perhaps none of us can find words to do justice to him who knew and loved words so much. He loved quotations too. He had a rich store of them, and he was them well. And so perhaps we can end with a quotation here tonight. His life was gentle. And the elements so mixed in him that nature might stand up and say to all the world, this was a man.

The gale of the world, just a phrase, perhaps, but one of those phrases that Matt Halton could

There was always something there in every one of his reports, a paragraph, perhaps a sentence,
